_all_text: Celestite celestite SrSO4 Pure SrSO4 is colorless to white; the characteristic pale blue color comes from color centers related to trace impurities or radiation effects. Strontium is one of the elements that gives a brilliant crimson red in flame tests and fireworks. Celestite is the primary commercial source of strontium compounds. Madagascar celestite geodes — hollow limestone nodules lined with pale-blue tabular crystals — are among the most widely sold mineral specimens globally. The celestial blue color is gentle and consistent. Crystals often have a translucent quality with pearly luster on cleavage surfaces. The name 'celestite' or 'celestine' comes from Latin 'caelestis' (heavenly) — a reference to the pale sky-blue color. The element strontium was first isolated from celestite and strontianite found near Strontian, a village in Argyll, Scotland — giving the element its name. Madagascar's Sakoany celestite geodes have supplied the global collector market for decades and are among the most recognizable mineral specimens in gift shops worldwide. heaven and the sky celestial communication angelic connection clarity and peace Primary strontium ore — industrial source of strontium compounds Strontium in fireworks (brilliant crimson-red flame) Strontium for red traffic lights and signal flares Strontium carbonate for CRT television glass (historical) Medical uses (strontium ranelate for osteoporosis) Collector mineral specimens celestite celestine strontium sulfate blue celestite Madagascar geode SrSO4 celestial blue mineral strontium ore sulfate strontium blue geode orthorhombic sedimentary madagascar collector-mineral celestial-blue heaven-name sky-crystal-cave Madagascar-geode strontium-sky pale-blue colorless white
